coredata.dc:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots incorporated in magnetite iron oxide nanoparticles for potential targeted brain cancer therapy: In -Vitro study
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Yaqoob, Zahida
Batool, Syeda Ammara
Abdul Basit, Muhammad
Konain, Kiran
Malik, Rizwan Ahmed
Rehman, Saeed Ur
Hussain, Syed Wilayat
Alrobei, Hussein
Ur Rehman, Muhammad Atiq
coredata.dc:description: Surface modification of magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles (NPs) using carbon dots (CDs) is a promising approach in biomedical applications due to excellent biocompatibility. Herein, the CDs were incorporated in the Fe3O4 NPs to observe the performance of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs for targeted brain cancer therapy. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealed the morphology of Fe3O4 NPs is rough and agglomerated. X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffraction pattern exhibits that the CDs are successfully incorporated in Fe3O4 NPs. Furthermore, magnetic properties evaluated using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) exhibited significant magnetization (51.362 emu/g) attributed to the greater occupancy of Fe+2 ions in the crystal lattice. XRD pattern confirmed the formation of NPs. Furthermore, functional groups of the NPs were validated through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The measurement of zeta potential affirmed the stability of the as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs, indicating a value of −7.64 mV, −0.5754 mV and −3.881 mV, respectively. Fe3O4 NPs exhibited a surface area of 58.6 m2/g as determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. The anticancer activity of the synthesized CDs-Fe3O4 NPs demonstrated inhibition against MG-U87 cells. Therefore, the synthesized CDs-Fe3O4 NPs hold potential for application in brain cancer therapy. In addition, CDs-Fe3O4 NPs exhibited exceptional biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity against osteoblast cells, establishing them as a safe biomaterial for biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Magnetite nanoparticles
Magnetic behavior
Brain cancer therapy
Biocompatible

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The experimental protocol of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs.
(a) FE SEM showed crystal appearance of Fe3O4 NPs (b) Elemental area mapping of Fe3O4 NPs (c) FE SEM of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs (d) Elemental area mapping of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs.
EDX analysis of (a) Fe3O4 NPs (b) CDs (c) CDs-Fe3O4 NPs, respectively.
A wide scan XPS spectrum of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs (a) survey spectrum (b) Fe 2p peaks (c) multiple peaks (d) O 1s.
UV–visible absorption spectrum and Tauc plot (a, b) Fe3O4 NPs, (c, d) CDs (e, f) CDs- Fe3O4 NPs with band gap energies of as-prepared NPs.
Colloidal stability of as-synthesized NPs using zeta potential for Fe3O4 NPs, CDs and CDs-Fe3O4 NP, respectively.
XRD spectra of Fe3O4 NPs (JCPDS No. 26–1136), CDs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs.
FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs- Fe3O4 NPs.
Hysteresis curve of (a) Fe3O4 NPs (b) CDs-Fe3O4 NPs with photographs of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs solution formed in response to external magnetic field (left) with magnet (right) without magnet.
(a) Type-IV isotherm with a wide hysteresis loop (b) the pore size distribution of the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs.
Overall survival fraction (%) of NPs against the MG-U87 cells and illustrations of the mechanism of Fenton-like reaction for tumor ablation.
WST-8 assay of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs against osteoblast cells incubated for 48 h.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of carbon dots incorporated in magnetite iron oxide nanoparticles for potential targeted brain cancer therapy: In -Vitro study

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Surface modification of magnetite (Fe3O4) nanoparticles (NPs) using carbon dots (CDs) is a promising approach in biomedical applications due to excellent biocompatibility. Herein, the CDs were incorporated in the Fe3O4 NPs to observe the performance of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs for targeted brain cancer therapy. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images revealed the morphology of Fe3O4 NPs is rough and agglomerated. X-ray diffraction (XRD) diffraction pattern exhibits that the CDs are successfully incorporated in Fe3O4 NPs. Furthermore, magnetic properties evaluated using vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) exhibited significant magnetization (51.362 emu/g) attributed to the greater occupancy of Fe+2 ions in the crystal lattice. XRD pattern confirmed the formation of NPs. Furthermore, functional groups of the NPs were validated through Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The measurement of zeta potential affirmed the stability of the as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs, indicating a value of −7.64 mV, −0.5754 mV and −3.881 mV, respectively. Fe3O4 NPs exhibited a surface area of 58.6 m2/g as determined by Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis. The anticancer activity of the synthesized CDs-Fe3O4 NPs demonstrated inhibition against MG-U87 cells. Therefore, the synthesized CDs-Fe3O4 NPs hold potential for application in brain cancer therapy. In addition, CDs-Fe3O4 NPs exhibited exceptional biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity against osteoblast cells, establishing them as a safe biomaterial for biomedical applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Brain tumors are among the deadliest of all cancers, with Glioblastoma being the most dangerous type of brain cancer. This form of cancer claims the lives of over two-thirds of adults within two years after diagnosis [1]. However, cancer can be treated through various methods, including radiation therapy, surgery, and chemotherapy [2]. Brain-targeted drug delivery is one of the most challenging domains of research. The presence of a blood-brain barrier, organized by microvascular endothelial cells at the blood to brain interface, is one of the main causes [3]. With the advancements in nanotechnology, which is extensively used in cancer research today, promising results have been achieved in the diagnosis and treatment of cancer [4]. Moreover, magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have exhibited exponential growth in the biomedical field, especially in drug administration, hyperthermia, tissue engineering, theragnosis, bioseparation, and manipulation [5].

Biocompatible MNPs are encapsulated with drugs to sustain the drug release process while serving as drug delivery carriers. The use of superparamagnetic Fe3O4 NPs in medicine has produced encouraging results, such as cell separation and apoptosis [6,7]. Fe3O4 NPs are frequently utilized in magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) as a contrast agent, as a heating source in case of hyperthermia, and as a magnetic field-assisted radiation treatment for cancerous cells [8,9]. Furthermore, Fe3O4 NPs also exhibit unique magnetic characteristics, low toxicity, biodegradability, and great biocompatibility, which makes them valuable for drug delivery applications [10]. Fe3O4 NPs, widely recognized as a prominent ferrofluid, have been widely investigated in the biomedical field with a particular focus on their colloidal dispersion [11]. Various coating agents, such as quantum dots (QDs) [12], carbon hybrid materials [13], carbon dots (CDs) [14], and polyvinylpyrrolidone are employed to modify the surface of magnetite particles to augment their performance.

Fe3O4 MNPs have been combined with semiconducting QDs, polymers or organic dyes, noble and rare earth metals that offers advantages by protecting Fe3O4 against agglomeration and preventing instability [11,15,16]. However, these nanomaterials have certain limitations for practical applications such as potential toxicity and poor photostability of organic dyes, high cost of noble metal/rare earth, and water insolubility of QDs [14,17]. Recent research on tunable-emission fluorescentCDs or graphene nano-sheets as a novel kind of fluorescence marker used in nano-biotechnology [18] and widely considered as a better alternative over semiconductor QDs. Semiconductor QDs are toxic due to inherent heavy metals and use of organic dyes in processing of photo stability and optical brightness [14,19].

CDs are general term known as nano-sized photoluminescence carbon nanomaterials with a characteristic size less than 10 nm and spherical in appearance [20]. CDs can be coated on many NPs such as on Fe3O4 to improve the efficacy in photothermal therapy, dual-model imaging, and near-infrared light-responsive drug carrier [14,21,22]. Zheng et al. prepared self-targeting CDs derived from fluorescent l-aspartic acid (Asp) that target brain cancer glioma. The CDs-Asp exhibited a stronger fluorescent signal at the glioma site than in the normal brain, confirming their potential to cross blood-brain barrier and precisely target glioma tissue [23]. Hui et al. reported that the nanoparticles combining magnetic iron oxide and fluorescent CDs have been developed for dual-modal imaging, serving as both a near-infrared light-triggered drug carrier and a platform for photothermal therapy [18]. Raziyeh et al. developed Fe3O4 integrated carbon quantum dots (CQDs) nanocomposites for MRI, cancer cell labelling and targeted drug delivery. The mentioned reference observed that the anticancer drug (Doxorubicin) loaded on Fe3O4/CQDs offers a sustained and targeted drug delivery system, enhancing its therapeutic performance against in vitro MCF7 for breast cancer therapy [12]. However, compared to the reported literature, the main innovation of the present study of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs demonstrated the inhibition against glioma cell line (U87) for potential brain cancer therapy. To the best of our knowledge CDs-Fe3O4 NPs for targeted brain cancer ablation are not reported in the previous research.

In the present study, CDs incorporated in Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized through a hydrothermal approach for potential targeted brain cancer therapy. The morphology of CDs- Fe3O4 NPs is studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). X-ray diffraction (XRD) and X-ray photospectroscopy (XPS) studies are utilized to identify the deposition of CDs on Fe3O4 NPs. The magnetic property studied by vibrating sample magnetometer (VSM) revealed significant magnetization, which may be attributed to the presence of Fe+2 ions. Furthermore, CDs-Fe3O4 NPs exhibited low cytotoxicity against osteoblast cells. The newly prepared CDs-Fe3O4 NPs are economical, cytocompatible, exhibit anticancer properties, and have a great potential for targeted cancer treatment.

The following materials were used to develop CDs and Fe3O4 NPs; Ferrous chloride tetrahydrate (FeCl2. 4H2O) (Fe (II)), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3. 6H2O) (Fe (III)), l-Ascorbic acid (99 %), Ethanol, and deionized water.

Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized using the co-precipitation method as briefly discussed elsewhere [24]. The FeCl2 and FeCl3 solutions were prepared separately, and following ratio of solution was used (FeCl2:FeCl3 = 1:2). For maintaining pH in the range of 10–12, NaOH solution was added dropwise. The final solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min and washed twice with deionized water and ethanol to attain a pH of 7. Subsequently, the precipitates were dried at 60 °C for 24 h under a nitrogen environment.

CDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method as described by Cailotto et al. [25]. Briefly, ascorbic acid (1 g) was dissolved completely in 50 mL of deionized water. The prepared solution was autoclaved in Teflon-lined stainless steel at 180 °C for 8 h and allowed to cool at room temperature. The formation of CDs was attributed to brownish luminescent color of the solution. The solution was centrifuged at 7000 rpm to remove the black precipitate and supernatant was filtered through a 0.22 μm syringe. Finally, yellow colored CDs were obtained.

CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were synthesized via hydrothermal method reported in literature [14]. The Fe3O4 NPs (0.5 g) were dispersed in 10 mL of deionized water, and further CDs solution was added to it. Both the solutions were mixed in 1:1 ratio. Afterwards, the mixture was transferred into autoclave and maintained at 180 °C for 8 h, followed by cooling at room temperature. Finally, CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were washed with deionized water, absolute ethanol, and vacuum dried. The experimental protocol of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs is shown in Fig. 1 .

Field emission SEM equipped with energy dispersive spectroscopy (FESEM-EDS: Tescan-MIRA III) was used to evaluate the morphology of NPs. To identify the different phases of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs, the samples were subjected to XRD (Panalytical-PW3719) using CuKα radiation in the 2θ range of 20–80° (step size: 0.02). Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR: Nicolet Summit LITE) in a transmittance mode was used to identify the functional groups in each sample. The scan speed was 4 m/s, and 4000-400 cm−1 range was covered. The NPs were dispersed in deionized water, and the ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–Vis: Shimadzu-601) was performed by scanning from 200 to 400 nm at room temperature, recording the spectra in a 1 cm3 quartz cuvette. The VSM (LAKE SHORE, 7407) was employed to measure the magnetization at ambient temperature as a function of magnetic field strength (Oe). The XPS spectrum of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were measured by Thermo Scientific Escalab250xi for binding energy analysis. The zeta potential of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were calculated using ZETASIZER (Nanoseries Model ZEN 3600, Malvern Instruments). The surface area and pore size range for the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were calculated using the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) and Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) models (V-Sorb 2800, GOLD APP Instruments), respectively. Nitrogen (N2) adsorption-desorption isotherm was obtained at liquid N2 temperature (77K). Prior to analysis, 20 mg of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were treated at 200 °C for 4 h to remove any impurity from the surface.

The prepared Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were tested for anticancer activity against the commercial cell line MG-U87, which was derived from a patient with malignant glioma [26]. The cells were grown in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium: Nutrient Mixture F-12 (DMEM-F12) along with fetal bovine serum (FBS), and penicillin-streptomycin (pen-strep). Cells were incubated at 37 °C in a standard environment of 95 % humidity and 5 % CO2. The detachment of confluent cells from culture flask was done using trypsin-EDTA (0.25 %), after which they were placed in a 96-well plate and incubated for another 24 h. The prepared NPs were applied in concentrations of (0, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100, 200, and 400) μM the following day, and the plate was incubated for an additional 48 h. After incubation with the NPs, the cells were fixed using formalin and stained with crystal violet dye. Following staining, the absorbance was measured with a UV spectrophotometer.

To assess their cytotoxicity, 10 mg of all the NPs were immersed in 50 μL of Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) in a 24-well plate. The NPs were sterilized prior to further processing under UV radiation for 60 min. The cyto-compatibility of the Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs was preliminarily examined using osteoblast cells; MG-63 (CRL 1427, ATCC, Manassas, VA) through the WST-8 assay. Briefly, MG-63 cells were cultured in DMEM with pen-strep (1 vol %) and FBS (10 vol %) and incubated under standard conditions for 48 h until reaching 80–90 % confluence. Subsequently, 1 mL (10,000 cells/mL) of cultured cells was added to each well of the 24-well plate containing the synthesized particles. The culture medium was refreshed every 48 h. The absorbance at specified time intervals was recorded using a microplate reader (Anthos-Phomo) at 460 nm. The tissue culture plate was established as positive control (100 % cell viability), and the cell viability of NPs were normalized against it.

SEM micrographs and elemental area maps of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were obtained, as shown in Fig. 2 . SEM images of Fe3O4 NPs clearly depict that the particles are agglomerated and spherical in appearance (Fig. 2a). Raziyeh et al. observed similar kind of morphological results [12]. The elemental area map of Fe3O4 NPs contained Fe and O as revealed in Fig. 2 (b). Fig. 2 (c) depicts that the CDs caused a significant alteration in Fe3O4 NPs agglomeration., The agglomeration of these semi-spherical NPs was observed in Fig. 2 (c), resulting from magnetic interaction and small particle size. Similarly, the distortions in the carbon microstructure are caused by the intercalation of iron atoms that is not seen clearly from the SEM image which is also discussed in the literature [12,13]. EDS-based elemental area map of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs in Fig. 2 (d) indicates the incorporation of CDs with the main elements C, Fe, and O. It would be interesting to conduct HRTEM in the future to confirm the deposition of CDs.

The EDS for Fe3O4 NPs, CDs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs is shown in Fig. 3 . The Fe3O4 NPs contains Fe and O. Similarly, Fig. 3 (b) shows that the CDs possess C, O, Na, and Mg. In contrast, the EDS of CDs deposited on Fe3O4 NPs (Fig. 3 (c)) shows the presence of Fe, O, and C, indicating that NPs in the shell possess large amount of C and Fe. This may be the elevated O/C atomic ratio exhibiting a substantial presence of oxygen-containing functional groups in the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs.

XPS was conducted to analyze the surface groups and composition of the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. The state of the elements and their bonds in the nanocomposite were assessed through XPS. The results are depicted in Fig. 4 . The survey spectrum reveals the presence of C, O, and Fe in the characterized sample, as illustrated in Fig. 4 (a). In Fig. 4b, the high resolution of Fe2p spectrum show two strong peaks. The peak at 707.3 eV attributes to Fe 2p3/2, and the 721.3 eV signal corresponds to Fe 2p1/2. The peaks are further divided in to three peaks such as 707.2, 709.1 and 721.2 eV, implying that the revealed peaks ranging from (705.5–711.5) eV attributed to Fe 2p3/2, while the peaks at higher binding values (718.9–724.9) eV attributed to Fe 2p1/2 [27]. The broadening and lower binding of shifted peaks is due to the occurrence of Fe+2 and Fe+3, which is consistent with the literature [28]. In Fig. 4 (b), considering that the Fe 2p3/2 peak closer to 709.2 eV corresponds to FeO, whereas at 710.8 eV it pertains to Fe2O3. We deduce that the deposited oxide is a combination of FeO and Fe2O3 NPs. Multiple peaks were observed for C 1s at approximately ∼289.5 eV, ∼287.5 eV, and ∼285 eV, corresponding to C=O, C–O–C/C–OH, and C–C bonds, respectively as shown in Fig. 4 (c) [23,29]. Moreover, the O 1s spectra in Fig. 4 (d) were fitted with two peaks which were observed at 530.2 eV (C–O–Fe), and 528.6 eV (Fe–O) respectively [30]. These results confirm the existence of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. The similar results of Fe3O4/CNDs prepared through the same method [31] confirm the deposition of CDs on Fe3O4 NPs. The analysis of XPS data provides an additional confirmation of the simultaneous presence of CDs and Fe3O4 NPs in the hybrid composite under consideration. It is clear that the XPS data precisely correlates with the FTIR peaks (described in Section 3.6).

The UV–vis spectra of as-synthesized NPs are displayed in Fig. 5 , which show peaks at 268 nm, 290 nm and 272–377 nm for Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4, respectively. These results indicate that the first spectral peak corresponds to a π→π∗ electronic transition from the C=C bond while the second peak indicates an n→π∗ transition from the C=O bonds [32].

According to the aromatic sp2 C–C bond, there are peaks in the UV–vis absorption of CDs at 230–280 nm [33]. The absorbance spectrum of CDs is shown in Fig. 5c. The n–π* transition of C=O or C=N and C–O or C–N bonds, or other relevant groups that might be responsible for a shoulder between 300 and 550 nm. The graphene quantum dots with a changed size are connected to shifts at the maximum of emission spectrum [34].

Moreover, these NPs displayed Tauc plots generated from the UV–vis spectra of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs in water dispersion as shown in Fig. 5 (b), (d) and (f). The direct band gap materials are often considered better than indirect band gap materials due to their more efficient interaction with light [35]. The bandgap energy of CDs is between 1.5 and 3.5 eV, as previously reported [36]. Hence, we found the band gap energy for Fe3O4 NPs to be 2.6 eV (Fig. 5b), CDs to be 3.02 eV (Fig. 5e), and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs to be 2.8 eV (Fig. 5f). The coated NPs demonstrate a bandgap value between that of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs.

The CDs dispersed in water exhibit a pronounced strong blue fluorescence emission under UV exposure (405 nm), which confirmed the formation of CDs. The optical images of CDs with the left image depicting CDs under the normal daylight exposure and the right image under UV exposure, as illustrated in Fig. 5d which highlight the size of NPs and the impact of quantum confinement during the transition of Fe3O4 NPs to CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. This transition enables the successful incorporation of fluorescence properties in the as-prepared CDs [37].

Fig. 6 illustrates the stability of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. The charge on all samples was measured in terms of the zeta potential. The zeta potential value (mV) of Fe3O4 NPs at pH = 7 was −7.64 mV. Generally, high stability (indicating a higher degree of dispersion or less agglomeration) is associated with zeta potential values below −25 mV or above +25 mV for NPs. The negative surface charge of Fe3O4 NPs at pH = 7 observed as −7.64 mV, may be attributed to the presence of carboxyl groups on the NPs surface [38]. Zeta potential values in the range of 0 to ±5 can lead to the precipitation of NPs suspension and rapid agglomeration.

The zeta potential value for CDs-Fe3O4 NPs is −3.881 mV, which can be explained by the tight binding of carboxylate groups from CDs with the surface of Fe3O4 NPs [11]. Moreover, the significant adsorption of CDs on the Fe3O4 NPs surface provides electrostatic contribution to the negative charge of the particles. However, the zeta potential of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs has not been reported in the literature. The zeta potential monitoring for Fe3O4 NPs confirmed the presence of carboxylate groups and negatively charged particles that is completely consistent with the XPS results. This demonstrates the stability of NPs is a crucial factor for biomedical applications.

The XRD patterns of the as-prepared Fe3O4 NPs, CDs-Fe3O4 NPs and CDs are presented in Fig. 7 . The diffraction peaks observed at 2θ = 30.08°, 35.43°, 37.06°, 53.41°, and 62.52°corresponded to the crystallographic planes (2 2 0), (3 1 1), (2 2 2), (4 2 2), and (4 4 0), respectively. These peaks align with the standard XRD pattern of Fe3O4 NPs (reference JCPDS No. 26–1136), confirming the crystalline nature of the synthesized Fe3O4 NPs through co-precipitation [39,40]. However, when compared to JCPDS card no. 26–1076, the XRD pattern of the as-prepared CDs (Fig. 7) revealed a broad diffraction peak at 24.05° and 37.06° corresponding to the miller index (002) and (400). This suggests that the space between the layers exceeds the size of the graphitic spaces, as does the width. Consequently, CDs exhibit a poor crystalline structure [41]. The application of Debye-Scherer equation determined the average crystallite size of the Fe3O4 NPs to be 56 nm [42]. This size falls within the desired range (less than 60 nm) for magnetic powders intended for biomedical applications. Furthermore, the XRD pattern of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs exhibited an abrupt change in intensity of peaks. Peaks at 30.08°, 35.43°, 37.06°, 53.41°, and 62.52° similar to the structure of Fe3O4 NPs. Also, appearance of mild peaks at 24.05° and 37.06° are associated with CDs.

The FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 NPs, CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs are presented in Fig. 8 . The FT-IR spectra of Fe3O4 NPs and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs show a tiny peak at 547 cm−1connected with the stretching vibrations of Fe–O at the quadrilateral and octahedral of Fe3O4 NPs, respectively [12]. We observed that, the band at 3356 cm−1disappear in the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs indicates that the Fe3O4 surface is functionalized to CDs. Hence, the peak confirmed the presence of Fe3O4 NPs and functionalization of CDs on Fe3O4 NPs.

There are multiple peaks in the FT-IR spectrum that are associated with the surface bonding of CDs. The stretching vibrations of the OH bond were represented by the absorption band that extended from 3356 to 3202 cm−1. These linkages may be the cause of the hydrophilicity of CDs and subsequent water dispersion. The stretching vibrations of C=O and C–H may be attributed to the minor peaks at 1634 cm−1 and 2088 cm−1, whereas the stretching vibration of the C–O group is linked to the peaks at 1335, and 942 cm−1 [43].

The magnetic properties of the prepared Fe3O4 and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were evaluated using VSM at room temperature. Fig. 9 (a) and 9 (b) illustrate the hysteresis curve of Fe3O4 and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs in a magnetic field at room temperature. It is obvious from the Fig. 9 that the remnant magnetization value was low because the value of magnetization did not achieve a constant limit. Due to which the negligible magnetic hysteresis is obtained that become clear by the transition of the hysteresis ring to an S-shaped curve, similar type of curve obtained from the previously reported value [44].

Moreover, the attraction of NPs towards the magnet confirms their magnetic properties, as shown in 9 (b). The S-shaped curve of magnetic hysteresis also demonstrates its ferromagnetic behaviour. The saturation magnetization of the Fe3O4 NPs is approximately 51.362 emu/g and coercivity around 52. 839 Oe. Similarly, the magnetic saturation of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs is 50.507 emu/g, with coercivity approximately 54 Oe, slightly lower than the Fe3O4 NPs. According to Mehdinia et al. [45] saturation magnetization (Ms) of the Fe3O4/CQD nanocomposite was approximately 43.27 emu/g. Raziyeh et al. [12] claim that magnetization exceeding 32 emu/g have the great capacity to attract a small magnet. As a result, it is anticipated that employing external magnet on CDs-Fe3O4 NPs will confer magnetic properties, facilitating easy separation from the reaction medium.

When designing magnetic NPs for biomedical applications, it is crucial to emphasize the significance of surface area because a larger surface area facilitates increased interaction sites between the particles and their surroundings, thereby offering more opportunities for cellular interactions [14]. The BET model was adopted to calculate the surface area of synthesized CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. Fig. 10 (a) shows the type-IV isotherm with a wide hysteresis loop. Type-IV isotherm indicates the mesoporosity (2–50 nm) that is present in the particles. The surface area was measured as 58.6 m2/g for the incorporated NPs, which is quite large. Fig. 10 (b) presents the distribution of pore size in the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. The highest portion of pore size obtained was 32 nm, which confirms the detection of mesoporosity in the NPs. A very small number of macropores (<50 nm) were also detected.

The anticancer activity of the prepared NPs was determined against MG-U87 glioma cancer cells by performing an in vitro cell growth inhibitory assay, as shown in Fig. 11 . The MG-U87 is an ATCC cell line derived from the malignant brain cancer type known as Glioblastoma Multiform (GBM). It is a grade four, aggressive tumor with a low survival rate. These cell lines are directly relevant to the disease under current study, allowing us to examine the efficiency and specificity of nanoparticles in targeting and killing the brain cancer cells. Using these cell lines also assures that the created nanoparticles can deliver the desired therapeutic effect, validating the anticancer activity of the prepared CDs prior to moving forward with in vivo studies and clinical trials.

Here, we observed that the concentration-dependent anticancer activity of NPs, where Fe3O4 NPs and CDs exhibited low inhibitory effect on cancer cells. The quantified results revealed the percentage of the cells surviving after 48 h of incubation at different concentrations (0–400 μM). The results identify that the Fe3O4 NPs increased within the concentration range of 6.25–100 μM and then decreased at higher concentration of 400 and 200 μM. For Fe3O4 NPs, the IC50 (half-maximum inhibitor concentration) value was calculated to be 464 ± 0.06 μΜ. Similarly, when cells were treated with the high concentration of CDs increased cells viability except at 400 μM post treatment. Quantification of these concentrations using UV spectrophotometry revealed an IC50 value for the CDs of 911 ± 0.05 μM. Interestingly, the combined effect of CDs-Fe3O4 NPs applied to the cancer cells line revealed a reduction in the amount of dye retained by the cells, indicating a decrease in the number of surviving cells. For U87-glioma cancer cells, the CDs-Fe3O4 NPs at a concentration of 400 μM inhibited the cancer cells. Computing these results on GraphPad prism showed that the IC50 value for CDs-Fe3O4 NPs was 263 ± 0.06 μM, indicating that CDs-Fe3O4 NPs could potentially be utilized as an anticancer agent. According to Zhang et al. [46] synthetic MNPs can be used as Fenton reagent (FR) for tumor treatment (in vitro and in vivo). The NPs were ionized under acidic tumors and then release (Fe2+ and Fe3+) because Fe ions can induce cell death under an oxidation-reduction reaction due to their potential to liberate reactive oxidative species (ROS), which are predicated on the conversion of less harmful H2O2 to cytotoxic OH under tumor microenvironment, and cancer cells are destroyed using Fenton reactions. Moreover, it was discovered that OH− generation using free Fe2+ ions occurred more quickly than Fe2+on the surface of Fe3O4 NPs [47,48]. The schematic illustration of Fenton reactions is shown in Fig. 11.

The goal of using MNPs is to specifically destroy cancer cells while sparing healthy cells. In Photodynamic therapy (PDT), when using a photosensitizer, exposure to light may also produce ROS, which can cause cell death. An increase in cell death in the presence of light would suggest that the PDT is working [49]. If MG-U87 cells exhibit high cytotoxicity in the presence of light, this could indicate that PDT may be effective in targeting cancer cells. If normal cells exhibit negligible cytotoxicity in the same conditions, the experiment is considered safe. In dark conditions, low cytotoxicity suggests that the treatment is safe and does not damage healthy cells. The optimal result is a balance where the treatment kills cancer cells efficiently in the presence of light and has little effect on healthy cells in dark conditions.

WST-8 assay was employed to assess the reaction of human MG-63 osteoblast cells to NPs over a period of up to six days in culture. Fig. 12 illustrates the determination of the osteoblast cells viability following a 6 d incubation period. During the 4 d of testing, the OD values of all the NPs under control increased with time of cultivation period. The osteoblast cells viability on CDs-Fe3O4 NPs exhibits a significant increase at day 4; however, the subsequent rise in values on day 6 is minimal, suggesting support for the process of bone mineralization. The results align closely with the previous report on Fe3O4–MBG. The biocompatibility of NPs depend on their concentration, chemical composition, size and shape [50,51]. Moreover, the nature of the surface functional group is largely determined by the cytotoxicity of NPs.

CDs have the ability to swiftly neutralize detrimental free radicals responsible for oxidative stress due to their hydroxyl structure. This make them highly valuable for cell shielding. Moreover, Lu et al. reported that one-step hydrothermal method to produce biocompatible CDs enriched with numerous OH groups. Thus, the resulting OH groups display robust fluorescence, excellent photo stability, and minimal cytotoxicity [52,53]. There was a negligible decrease in the cell viability after 6 days. The presence of Fe3O4 NPs resulted in enhanced cell growth and activity in the presence of weak electromagnetic field of cells. This improvement was attributed to the generation of magnetic fields that facilitated improved communication among cells reported by Maleki-Ghaleh., H et al. [54]. The CDs-Fe3O4 NPs were utilized in cell culture, and alterations in their surface groups and elemental composition were analyzed using FTIR and XPS. These results confirm that the as-prepared CDs-Fe3O4 NPs are highly biocompatible due to the considerable increase in cell proliferation.

A straightforward and facile synthesis route was carried out to develop highly stable and magnetic CDs-Fe3O4 NPs through a hydrothermal method. Moreover, the prepared CDs were synthesized using an environmentally friendly and green method. The structural characteristics of the synthesized NPs were comprehensively characterized using XRD, XPS, FE-SEM, BET, zeta Potential, FT-IR, VSM, and UV–vis. The XRD spectra of Fe3O4 NPs confirmed their exceptional crystallinity that highlight a cubic spinel structure as compared to CDs with poor crystalline nature. The FT-IR analysis confirmed the presence of the carboxylate group in both the CDs, and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs. Both Fe3O4 and CDs-Fe3O4 NPs are particularly suitable for brain cancer therapy as they exhibit magnetic properties within the MRI detectable range. The BET analysis revealed a substantial increase in surface area that enhanced the biomedical applications of NPs. The combining properties of magnetism, anticancer activity and cytocompatibility in the CDs-Fe3O4 indicate the promising applications, especially in cancer therapies. Additionally, their suitability for osteogenic applications further broadens their potential biomedical use. The fluorescent and biocompatible nanocarriers such as the prepared CDs-Fe3O4 NPs may be able to demonstrate the ability to traverse the blood brain barrier. The CDs-Fe3O4 NPs are applicable as biocompatible nano-carriers with drug fluorescence that are anticipated to offer innovative solutions for clinical development of brain cancer drugs. Their high surface area, small size, biocompatibility, fluorescence, and magnetic properties make these NPs ideal for the treatment of brain tumors.

Zahida Yaqoob: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Syeda Ammara Batool: Writing – original draft, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Muhammad Abdul Basit: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Kiran Konain: Resources, Methodology, Data curation. Rizwan Ahmed Malik: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Data curation. Saeed Ur Rehman: Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Syed Wilayat Hussain: Visualization, Resources, Methodology, Investigation. Hussein Alrobei: Resources, Investigation, Data curation. Muhammad Atiq Ur Rehman: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study is supported via funding from Prince Sattam bin Abdulaziz University project number (PSAU/2024/R/1445).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Magnetic mesoporous bioactive glass for synergetic use in bone regeneration, hyperthermia treatment, and controlled drug delivery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. S. Ur Rahman et al., “Magnetic mesoporous bioactive glass for synergetic use in bone regeneration, hyperthermia treatment, and controlled drug delivery,” RSC Adv., vol. 10, no. 36, pp. 21413-21419, 2020, doi: 10.1039/c9ra09349d.
A. Magrez et al., “Cellular Toxicity of Nanomaterials,” Am. Chem. Soc., vol. 6, no. 6, pp. 1121-1125, 2006.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydroxyl functionalized carbon dots with strong radical scavenging ability promote cell proliferation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: F. Lu et al., “Hydroxyl functionalized carbon dots with strong radical scavenging ability promote cell proliferation,” Mater. Res. Express, vol. 6, no. 6, 2019, doi: 10.1088/2053-1591/ab0c55.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Influence of Fe3O4 nanoparticles in hydroxyapatite scaffolds on proliferation of primary human fibroblast cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Maleki-Ghaleh et al., “Influence of Fe3O4 Nanoparticles in Hydroxyapatite Scaffolds on Proliferation of Primary Human Fibroblast Cells,” J. Mater. Eng. Perform., vol. 25, no. 6, pp. 2331-2339, 2016, doi: 10.1007/s11665-016-2086-4.
